food_name: Fresh Mixed Fruit or Vegetable Juice
aliases: Fresh Mixed Fruit or Vegetable Juice

english: Fresh Mixed Fruit or Vegetable Juice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers fruit juice broadly; no specific mixed fruit-vegetable juice term exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226365003 covers fruit juice broadly; used as closest parent match for mixed fruit-vegetable juice.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one glass (approximately 250 ml / 250 g), providing about 65 kcal and 13.75 g of carbohydrates.

nutrient_source.title: Fresh Mixed Fruit or Vegetable Juice — Singapore HPB Foodo Database (CR ID D03040009)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record D03040009 for Fresh mixed fruit or vegetable juice. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a generic mixed fruit-vegetable juice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 43 is estimated based on the published GI for apple juice (GI ~41, University of Sydney) and orange juice (GI ~50, University of Sydney), averaged and adjusted downward to reflect the vegetable component diluting the sugar load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (43 × 13.75) / 100 = 5.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (40 mg/100 g) with no added salt; potassium from fruit and vegetable content may support blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Fresh mixed fruit-vegetable juice contains only 40 mg sodium per 100 g and is free of added salt, making it acceptable for hypertension management; however, individuals should favour vegetable-dominant blends (e.g., celery, cucumber, spinach) to maximise potassium intake and minimise natural fruit sugar load, and should avoid adding honey or syrups.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Liquid sugar (4.16 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.46 g/100 g) results in rapid glucose absorption despite a moderate GI of 43
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit fresh mixed juice to small portions (100–150 ml) due to the low fibre content after straining, which removes the fibre buffer that would otherwise slow glucose absorption; blending without straining (smoothie style) or increasing the vegetable-to-fruit ratio is strongly preferred to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.13 g/100 g), negligible saturated fat, and low energy density (26 kcal/100 g) with antioxidant micronutrients from fruit and vegetable sources
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fresh mixed fruit-vegetable juice is low in total fat and saturated fat, contributing negligible cardiovascular risk from lipid load; the antioxidant vitamins (vitamin C, carotenoids) present in the juice may offer modest cardioprotective benefit, but whole fruit and vegetables are preferred over juice to retain fibre and maximise cardiovascular benefit.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Provides vitamin C and antioxidants but lacks omega-3 fatty acids, polyphenol-rich components, and dietary fibre due to straining
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fresh mixed juice provides some antioxidant benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management, but the removal of fibre and pulp during juicing reduces anti-inflammatory polyphenol delivery; retaining pulp or adding ginger and turmeric to the blend would meaningfully enhance anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Mixed Fruit or Vegetable Juice is a freshly prepared beverage made by pressing or blending a combination of multiple fruits and/or vegetables and straining out the pulp to yield a liquid drink. Common ingredient combinations include apple, orange, carrot, celery, cucumber, spinach, beetroot, and ginger, though the exact composition varies widely by vendor and preparation. The juice is typically served chilled and has a light, refreshing flavour profile that ranges from sweet-tart (fruit-dominant blends) to earthy and mildly bitter (vegetable-dominant blends). The texture is smooth and liquid, with no significant pulp when strained. Nutritionally, per 100 g the juice provides approximately 26 kcal, 0.45 g protein, 0.13 g fat, 5.5 g carbohydrates (of which 4.16 g are sugars), 0.46 g dietary fibre, 40 mg sodium, 11 mg calcium, and 0.55 mg iron. The low energy density and low sodium content make it broadly suitable as a hydrating beverage, but the low fibre content (due to straining) means sugars are absorbed more rapidly than in whole fruit or vegetables. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 43 (low-moderate), with a glycemic load of approximately 5.9 per 250 ml serving. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit fresh mixed juice to small portions or prefer blended (unstrrained) preparations to retain fibre. For hypertension, vegetable-dominant blends are preferred to maximise potassium and minimise sugar. For cardiovascular disease, the very low fat and saturated fat content is favourable. This food is classified as a minimally processed beverage in the 100% Juices subgroup and is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free dietary patterns.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fresh mixed fruit and vegetable juice?
Can diabetics drink fresh mixed juice?
Is mixed juice good for high blood pressure?
What is the sugar content of fresh mixed juice?
Is fresh juice better than whole fruit?
How much fibre is in fresh mixed juice?
What is the GI of mixed fruit juice?
Can I drink mixed juice if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record D03040009 at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database reference values for apple juice and orange juice, clearly documented. All hard gates satisfied.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; fresh fruit-vegetable juice contains negligible saturated fat). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based beverage contains no cholesterol). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this generic mixed juice record are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D03040009 as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI value of 43 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for apple juice (~41) and orange juice (~50); no direct GI measurement exists for a generic mixed fruit-vegetable juice. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (43 × 13.75) / 100 ≈ 5.9. 4. The input record cited a GI of 15 from an 'Embedded GI Database' at confidence 0.375 — this source is not on the approved list and the value is implausibly low for a juice with 5.5 g/100 g carbohydrates; it has been replaced with the analogous estimate of 43 from the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. This is a generic record; actual nutrient composition will vary significantly depending on the specific fruits and vegetables used and their proportions. 6. FoodOn match is 'closest_parent' (FOODON_03301710 fruit juice liquid); no specific mixed fruit-vegetable juice term exists in FoodOn as of 2026-04-16. SNOMED CT 226365003 used as 'closest_parent' match.
